question: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave is given by E→=E0x^+y^sinkz-ωt. Its magnetic field will be given by

q: The magnetic field of a plane electromagnetic wave is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The amplitude of the electric field would be
question: The magnetic field of a plane electromagnetic wave is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The amplitude of the electric field would be
solution:
The ratio of electric field amplitude to the magnetic field amplitude of an electromagnetic wave is equal to the speed of light in the medium. Therefore,

c=E0B0⇒E0=cB0

⇒E0=3×108×2×10-8

⇒E0=6 V m-1

According to the given equation B→=2×10-8sin0.5×103x+1.5×1011tj^ T the wave travels in x direction. As electric field and magnetic field vibrate perpendicular to the velocity of light, electric field can not be along x axis.

subject: Physics
q:
The electric field of an electromagnetic wave travelling through a medium is given by
$\vec{E}(x, t)=25 \sin \left(2.0 \times 10^{15} t-10^{7} x\right) \hat{n}$
then the refractive index of the medium is $\_\_\_\_$.
(All given measurement are in SI units)
question:
The electric field of an electromagnetic wave travelling through a medium is given by
$\vec{E}(x, t)=25 \sin \left(2.0 \times 10^{15} t-10^{7} x\right) \hat{n}$
then the refractive index of the medium is $\_\_\_\_$.
(All given measurement are in SI units)
solution:
The electric field is $E(x,t) = 25\sin(2.0 \times 10^{15}t - 10^7 x)$ V/m.
This is in the standard form $E = E_0\sin(\omega t - kx)$ where angular frequency $\omega = 2.0 \times 10^{15}$ rad/s and wave number $k = 10^7$ rad/m.
The speed of the wave in the medium is $v = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{15}}{10^7} = 2.0 \times 10^8$ m/s.
The refractive index is defined as $n = \frac{c}{v}$ where $c = 3.0 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light in vacuum.
Therefore, $n = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8}{2.0 \times 10^8} = 1.5$

subject: Physics
q:
The intensity of the light from a bulb incident on a surface is 0.22 W m-2. The amplitude of the magnetic field in this light-wave is _____ ×10-9 T

(Given : Permittivity of vacuum ϵ0=8.85×10-12 C2 N-1 m-2, speed of light in vacuum c=3×108 m s-1)
question:
The intensity of the light from a bulb incident on a surface is 0.22 W m-2. The amplitude of the magnetic field in this light-wave is _____ ×10-9 T

(Given : Permittivity of vacuum ϵ0=8.85×10-12 C2 N-1 m-2, speed of light in vacuum c=3×108 m s-1)
solution:
The intensity is defined as energy per unit time per unit area. Therefore, I=dEAdt.

Energy density is energy per unit volume. Therefore, Ud=dEAdx.

Dividing IUd=dxdt=c

⇒Ud=Ic=0.223×108=223×10-10 J m-3

Magnetic energy density will be half of total energy density, Therefore, Ub=113×10-10 J m-3.

Now energy density in magnetic field is given by, Ub=B22μ0. Therefore,

Brms22μ0=113×10-10 J m-3 where Brms2=B02=B022

⇒B024μ0=113×10-10⇒B0=4×4π×10-7×113×10-10⇒B0=4.29×10-8 T≃43×10-9 T

subject: Physics
q: A monochromatic source of light operating at $15$ kW emits $2.5\times 10^{22}$ photons/s. The region of an electromagnetic spectrum to which the emitted electromagnetic radiation belongs to ________. (Take $h=6.6\times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c=3\times 10^8$ m/s).
question: A monochromatic source of light operating at $15$ kW emits $2.5\times 10^{22}$ photons/s. The region of an electromagnetic spectrum to which the emitted electromagnetic radiation belongs to ________. (Take $h=6.6\times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c=3\times 10^8$ m/s).
solution:
Power of the source, $P = 15 \text{ kW} = 15 \times 10^3 \text{ J/s}$

Number of photons emitted per second, $n = 2.5 \times 10^{22} \text{ s}^{-1}$

Energy of one photon, $E = \dfrac{P}{n} = \dfrac{15 \times 10^3}{2.5 \times 10^{22}} = 6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

Using $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$, we get $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^{-19}} = 3.3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 330 \text{ nm}$

The wavelength $330 \text{ nm}$ lies in the ultraviolet region of the electromagnetic spectrum.

Answer: Ultraviolet

q: The electric field of a plane polarized electromagnetic wave in free space at time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is given by the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The magnetic field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>z</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is given by (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is the velocity of light.)
question: The electric field of a plane polarized electromagnetic wave in free space at time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is given by the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The magnetic field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>z</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is given by (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is the velocity of light.)
solution:
E=E0cosωt-K  →· r →

∴ At any time t,

K→=6i^+8k^

B0=E0c=10c

λ=2πk=2π10=π5

Also, E→×B→=c→

∴ω=2πf=2πcλ

i^j^k^010BxByBz=6i^+8k^10

ω=2π×cπ5=10c

∴i^Bz-0+j^0+k^-Bx=0.6i^-0.8k^

Comparing the coefficients on both sides,

∴Bz=0.6; Bx=-0.8

B0→=10c-0.8i^+0.6k^=-8i^+6k^c

So the magnetic field,B→=1c-8i^+6k^cos⁡6x+8z-10ct tesla

The ratio of the force acting on charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is :
question: The magnetic field vector of an electromagnetic wave is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfrac><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> represents unit vector along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis respectively. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo></math> two electric charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi></math> coulomb and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></math> coulomb located at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi>k</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mi>k</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, have the same velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is the velocity of light ). The ratio of the force acting on charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is :
solution:
At t=0
B at 0, 0, πk=B0i^+j^2cosπ
B at 0, 0, 3πk=B0i^+j^2cos3π
Force on charged particle q1
F1=q1V→1×B→1
=4π0.5ci^×-B0i^+j^2=4πB0c22-k^
Force on charged particle q2
F2=q2V→2×B→2
=2π0.5ci^×-B0i^+j^2=2πB0c22-k^⇒F1F2=2

solution:

After analysing each option, we get option B is the correct option

as E→B→=602×10-7=3×108 m s-1.

subject: Physics
q:
Match List - I with List - II.
$\begin{array}{l} \text{List - I} & \text{List - II} \\ \text{Relation} & \text{Law} \\ \text{A. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=-\frac{d}{d t} \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d a} & \text{I. Ampere's circuital law} \\ \text{B. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0}\left(I+\epsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}\right) & \text{II. Faraday's laws of} \\ & \text{electromagnetic induction} \\ \text{C. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d a}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \int_{\mathrm{V}} \rho \mathrm{dv} & \text{III. Ampere - Maxwell law} \\ \text{D. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I & \text{IV. Gauss's law of electrostatics} \end{array}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List - I with List - II.
$\begin{array}{l} \text{List - I} & \text{List - II} \\ \text{Relation} & \text{Law} \\ \text{A. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=-\frac{d}{d t} \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d a} & \text{I. Ampere's circuital law} \\ \text{B. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0}\left(I+\epsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}\right) & \text{II. Faraday's laws of} \\ & \text{electromagnetic induction} \\ \text{C. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d a}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \int_{\mathrm{V}} \rho \mathrm{dv} & \text{III. Ampere - Maxwell law} \\ \text{D. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I & \text{IV. Gauss's law of electrostatics} \end{array}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Theoretical
A-II, B-III, C-IV, D-I

A. $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt} \oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{a}$ → Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction (II)
B. $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 \left(I + \epsilon_0 \frac{d\Phi_E}{dt}\right)$ → Ampere-Maxwell law (III)
C. $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{a} = \frac{1}{\epsilon_0} \int \rho \, dv$ → Gauss's law of electrostatics (IV)
D. $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I$ → Ampere's circuital law (I)

q:

The corresponding magnetic field B→ is then given by:
question:
The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave is given by

The corresponding magnetic field B→ is then given by:
options: <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfrac><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>)</mo></math>

<math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfrac><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>)</mo></math>

<math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfrac><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>)</mo></math>

<math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfrac><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
E→=E0 i^ cos kz cosωt
E→=E02 i^ cos kz-ωt+E02i^ coskz+ωt
B→=E02C j^ cos kz-ωt+E02Cj^ coskz+ωt
=E02Cj^ 2 sin kzsinωt
=E0Cj^  sin kzsinωt

subject: Physics
q:
Match List I and List II

  List I   List II
A Microwaves I Physiotherapy
B UV rays II Treatment of cancer
C Infra-red rays III Lasik eye surgery
D X-rays IV Aircraft navigation

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
question:
Match List I and List II

  List I   List II
A Microwaves I Physiotherapy
B UV rays II Treatment of cancer
C Infra-red rays III Lasik eye surgery
D X-rays IV Aircraft navigation

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
solution:
A. Microwave → IV

Microwaves have short wavelength hence low energy dispersion during long distance communication. Thus, they are used in radar system.
B. UV rays → III

LASIK uses an excimer laser (an ultraviolet laser) to remove a thin layer of corneal tissue.

C. Infra-red →I

Infrared radiation in the device called infrared lamp is used in physiotherapy. This light is useful in reducing pain, decreasing inflammation, and speeding up wound care and tissue repair.


D. X-ray → II

Radiation therapy uses high-energy particles or waves, such as x-rays to destroy or damage cancer cells.

question: An electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>GHz</mi></math>, is travelling in a medium whose relative electric permittivity and relative magnetic permeability both are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Its velocity in this medium is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Given : Frequency of wave f=5GHz

=5×109 Hz

Relative permittivity, ϵr=2 and Relative permeability, μr=2 Since speed of light in a medium is given by,

v=1μ∈=1μrμ0·ϵr∈0

v=1μrϵf1μ0ϵ0=Cμrϵr

Where C is speed of light is vacuum

∴ v=3×1084=30×1072 m s-1

=15×107 m s-1

∴ Ans. is 15

It produces continuous as well as characteristic <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>rays. If <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub></math> is the smallest possible wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>ray in the spectrum, the variation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub></mfenced></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfenced><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> is correctly represented in :
question: An electron beam is accelerated by a potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> to hit a metallic target to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>rays. It produces continuous as well as characteristic <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>rays. If <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub></math> is the smallest possible wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>ray in the spectrum, the variation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub></mfenced></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfenced><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> is correctly represented in :
solution:
K.E.=eV,

KE=The kinetic energy of electron

The maximum energy of photon=K.E.

eV=hCλmin

⇒λmin V=hCe=constant

Taken log in both sides

lnλmin+lnV=lnconstant

⇒lnλmin=-lnV+ln(constant)

y=-ax+c the straight line of negative slope.

subject: Physics
q:
The equation of the electric field of an electromagnetic wave propagating through free space is given by : $E=\sqrt{377} \sin \left(6.27 \times 10^{3} t-2.09 \times 10^{-5} x\right) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{C}$
The average power of the electromagnetic wave is $\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}\right) \mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$
(Take $\sqrt{\frac{\mu_{0}}{\varepsilon_{o}}}=377$ in SI units)
question:
The equation of the electric field of an electromagnetic wave propagating through free space is given by : $E=\sqrt{377} \sin \left(6.27 \times 10^{3} t-2.09 \times 10^{-5} x\right) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{C}$
The average power of the electromagnetic wave is $\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}\right) \mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$
(Take $\sqrt{\frac{\mu_{0}}{\varepsilon_{o}}}=377$ in SI units)
solution:
From the electric field equation, amplitude:
$E_0 = \sqrt{377}$ N/C
Average intensity of EM wave:
$I_{avg} = \frac{E_0^2}{2Z_0}$ where $Z_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 377$ Ω
$I_{avg} = \frac{(\sqrt{377})^2}{2 \times 377} = \frac{377}{2 \times 377} = \frac{1}{2}$ W/m²
Given $I_{avg} = \frac{1}{\alpha}$ W/m², we have $\frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{2}$
Therefore, $\alpha = 2$

subject: Physics
q: A radio transmitter transmits at $830 \mathrm{kHz}$. At a certain distance from the transmitter magnetic field has amplitude $4.82 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~T}$. The electric field and the wavelength are respectively
question: A radio transmitter transmits at $830 \mathrm{kHz}$. At a certain distance from the transmitter magnetic field has amplitude $4.82 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~T}$. The electric field and the wavelength are respectively
solution:
Frequency of EM wave $v=830 \mathrm{KHz}$ $=830 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}$.
Magnetic field, $B=4.82 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~T}$
As we know, frequency, $v=\frac{c}{\lambda}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { or } \lambda & =\frac{c}{v}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{830 \times 10^3} \\
\lambda & \simeq 360 \mathrm{~m} \\
\text { And, } E & =B C=4.82 \times 10^{-11} \times 3 \times 10^8 \\
& =0.014 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

=9NC
So,

subject: Physics
q:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
\(\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\ \hline \text{A.} & \text{Radio-wave} & \text{I.} & \text{is produced by Magnetron valve} \\ \hline \text{B.} & \text{Micro-wave} & \text{II.} & \text{due to change in the vibrational modes of atoms} \\ \hline \text{C.} & \text{Infrared-wave} & \text{III.} & \text{due to inner shell electrons moving from higher to lower energy level} \\ \hline \text{D.} & \text{X-ray} & \text{IV.} & \text{due to rapid acceleration of electrons} \\ \hline \end{array}\)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
\(\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\ \hline \text{A.} & \text{Radio-wave} & \text{I.} & \text{is produced by Magnetron valve} \\ \hline \text{B.} & \text{Micro-wave} & \text{II.} & \text{due to change in the vibrational modes of atoms} \\ \hline \text{C.} & \text{Infrared-wave} & \text{III.} & \text{due to inner shell electrons moving from higher to lower energy level} \\ \hline \text{D.} & \text{X-ray} & \text{IV.} & \text{due to rapid acceleration of electrons} \\ \hline \end{array}\)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Matching electromagnetic wave sources:
Radio-wave (A) - produced by rapid acceleration of electrons in antennas and transmitters, matches IV.
Micro-wave (B) - produced by Magnetron valve, matches I.
Infrared-wave (C) - due to change in vibrational modes of atoms and molecules, matches II.
X-ray (D) - due to inner shell electrons moving from higher to lower energy levels, matches III.
Correct pairing: A-IV, B-I, C-II, D-III

subject: Physics
q:
A plane electromagnetic wave propagating in x-direction is described by
Ey=200 V m-1sin1.5×107t-0.05x; The intensity of the wave is :
(Use ϵ0=8.85×10-12 C2 N-1 m-2)
question:
A plane electromagnetic wave propagating in x-direction is described by
Ey=200 V m-1sin1.5×107t-0.05x; The intensity of the wave is :
(Use ϵ0=8.85×10-12 C2 N-1 m-2)
solution:
The magnitude of the electric field component of the electromagnetic wave is given by

E0=200 V m-1

The formula to calculate the intensity of the electromagnetic wave is given by

I=12ε0cE02   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

I=12×8.85×10-12×3×108×2002 W m-2=53.1 W m-2

subject: Physics
q: A plane electromagnetic wave is propagating along the direction <math><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> with its polarization along the direction <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>.</mo></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> The correct form of the magnetic field of the wave would be (here <math><msub><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> is an appropriate constant):
question: A plane electromagnetic wave is propagating along the direction <math><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> with its polarization along the direction <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>.</mo></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> The correct form of the magnetic field of the wave would be (here <math><msub><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> is an appropriate constant):
solution:
Polarization of EM Wave means Direction of Its electric Field

Electric field vector is along k^

So, unit electric filed vector, E→=k^ (Given)

Direction of wave,c^=i^+j^2  (Given)

B^  denotes unit magnetic field vector.

B^=C^ ×E^

B^=i^+j^2×k^=i^-j^2

General Form is B=(B0B^)cosωt-c^k

where B0 is Magnitude of Magnetic Field

So, B=B0i^-j^2cosωt-ki^+j^2

subject: Physics
q: The amplitude of magnetic field in an electromagnetic wave propagating along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The maximum value of electric field in the electromagnetic wave is
question: The amplitude of magnetic field in an electromagnetic wave propagating along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The maximum value of electric field in the electromagnetic wave is
solution:
The relation between the electric field and magnetic field is given by

E0 = cB0 .

The given data is 

B0=6×10-7 T

c=3×108 m s-1

Thus, the maximum value of the electric field is

E0= 3×108×6×10–7=180 V m-1.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Electromagnetic waves carry energy as they travel through space and this energy is equally shared by the electric and magnetic fields.

Statement II: When electromagnetic waves strike a surface, a pressure is exerted on the surface.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Electromagnetic waves carry energy as they travel through space and this energy is equally shared by the electric and magnetic fields.

Statement II: When electromagnetic waves strike a surface, a pressure is exerted on the surface.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
solution:
Electromagnetic wave carries energy shared equally in magnetic and electric field which is given by, =12ε0E2=B22μ0, as E=cB and c2=1μ0ε0.

Also, Electromagnetic wave carries momentum and therefore exerts force when it strikes a surface.

q: Sunlight of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math>is incident normally on the surface of a solar panel. question: Sunlight of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math>is incident normally on the surface of a solar panel. Some part of incident energy (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math>) is reflected from the surface and the rest is absorbed. The force exerted on <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> surface area will be close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
The pressure exerted by the photons

 P=0.75×Ic +0.25×2Ic ( P = pressure, c = speed of light, I = intensity )

P=1.25 ×Ic

 We know the force can be computed using

F=P×A 

( F = force, P = pressure, A = area )

F=1.25×50×13×108

F=20.83×10-8 N
